description: President Biden announced up to $20,000 in student loan forgiveness in August 2022. Overall support hovered around 55% but was deeply polarized along partisan lines (~80% Dem support, ~25% GOP support). Supreme Court struck it down in June 2023, but alternative relief programs continued.

key_events.description: Biden announces $10-20K student loan forgiveness plan (August 24, 2022). 43 million borrowers potentially eligible. Progressive wing wanted more; Republicans call it unfair.
26 million applications filed within weeks. Enthusiasm high among beneficiaries. Republican AGs from 6 states file lawsuits. GOP frames as transfer from non-college workers to graduates.
Federal courts block the program. 8th Circuit issues injunction. Political battle intensifies. Biden extends payment pause while fighting in courts.
Supreme Court hears oral arguments (February 2023). Conservative majority skeptical. Media coverage amplifies both sides. Opinion crystallizes along partisan lines.
Supreme Court strikes down plan 6-3 (June 2023). Biden announces alternative SAVE plan. NPR/Marist poll: 55% still support some form of forgiveness. Issue becomes 2024 campaign topic.

calibration_notes: Highly polarized issue with remarkably stable aggregate opinion despite intense debate. NPR/Marist and Pew polls consistently showed 55-60% support for some form of student loan relief. But the aggregate masked extreme partisan polarization (~80% D vs ~25% R). Very few undecideds — this was a pre-sorted issue. Tests: partisan pre-sorting limiting persuasion, aggregate stability masking polarization, legal/institutional intervention effects, motivated reasoning.
